title: Unit Testing
overview: Unit testing means testing one small unit of code — usually a single method or class — in complete isolation.
In a Java backend, that's typically one service method with all its dependencies replaced by fakes.
**JUnit 5** runs the tests. **Mockito** fakes the dependencies so the unit stays isolated.
why: A backend service almost always depends on other classes — repositories, gateways, other services.
If a test exercises the real dependencies too, it stops testing one unit and starts testing a whole chain.
Unit tests need a way to run fast, in isolation, and pinpoint exactly which class broke.
problem: Without isolation, testing `OrderService.placeOrder()` also runs the real `OrderRepository`, which hits a real database.
That test is now slow, needs a database connection, and can fail for reasons unrelated to `OrderService` itself.
**JUnit 5** solves *how to structure and run* the test.
**Mockito** solves *how to fake the dependencies* so only `OrderService`'s own logic is under test.

intro: JUnit 5 provides the test framework; Mockito provides the fake dependencies. Together they isolate one unit.

topics.heading: JUnit 5
points: **Core annotations** — `@Test`, `@BeforeEach`, `@AfterEach`, `@BeforeAll`, `@AfterAll` control setup and teardown around each test.
`@DisplayName` gives a test a readable name in reports. `@Disabled` skips a test without deleting it.
**Assertions** — `assertEquals`, `assertNotNull`, `assertThrows`, `assertAll` (groups multiple assertions), `assertTimeout`.
AssertJ's `assertThat(...)` is often preferred for its fluent, chainable API over plain JUnit assertions.
**Parameterized tests** — `@ParameterizedTest` runs the same test body against many inputs, supplied by `@ValueSource`, `@CsvSource`, `@MethodSource`, or `@EnumSource`.
**`@Nested`** inner classes group related tests by scenario or state, and still share setup from the outer class.
**Lifecycle** — by default JUnit creates a new test instance per method. `@TestInstance(Lifecycle.PER_CLASS)` shares one instance and its state across all methods.
`@TestMethodOrder` forces a specific execution order, for the rare case where test order actually matters.

topics.heading: Mockito
points: **Creating mocks** — `@Mock` plus `@ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class)`, or `Mockito.mock(PaymentGateway.class)` directly.
A fresh mock returns defaults for everything — `null`, `0`, `false` — until you stub it.
**Stubbing** — `when(gateway.charge(request)).thenReturn(receipt)` defines what a mock returns.
`thenThrow()` makes a mock throw an exception; `thenAnswer()` computes a dynamic response based on the arguments passed in.
**Verification** — `verify(repository).save(any(Order.class))` confirms an interaction actually happened.
`verify(repository, times(2)).findAll()` checks a call count; `verify(repository, never()).delete(any())` confirms something never happened.
**`ArgumentCaptor`** — `@Captor ArgumentCaptor<Order> captor` captures the actual argument passed to a mock method.
Used as `verify(repository).save(captor.capture())`, then `captor.getValue()` to inspect what was actually saved.
**`@Spy`** wraps a real object — real methods run unless explicitly stubbed. Useful for partial mocking.
Stubbing a spy requires `doReturn(value).when(spy).method()`, not the usual `when(...).thenReturn(...)` syntax.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Arrange: build request, stub gateway.charge()"] --> B["Act: call paymentService.pay(request)"]
    B --> C["Assert: verify returned Receipt"]
    C --> D["Verify: gateway.charge() was called once"]
    D --> E{"All assertions pass?"}
    E -->|Yes| F["Test green"]
    E -->|No| G["Test fails, points at PaymentService"]
architectureFlow.caption: The AAA flow for a unit test: Arrange the mocks and data, Act on the class under test, then Assert and Verify the outcome.

simpleExample.scenario: Testing a `PaymentService` that calls a `PaymentGateway` to charge a customer.
simpleExample.input: A `PaymentRequest` for order `order-1` worth 500, with `gateway.charge()` stubbed to return a successful `Receipt`.
simpleExample.execution: `paymentService.pay(request)` runs its own logic and delegates the charge to the mocked `gateway` — no real gateway or network call happens.
simpleExample.output: A `Receipt` with status `SUCCESS`, and Mockito confirms `gateway.charge(request)` was called exactly once.
simpleExample.explanation: The real `PaymentGateway` never runs. Only `PaymentService`'s own logic is exercised, so a failure here can only mean a bug in `PaymentService`.

realWorldExample.scenario: Testing an `OrderService.placeOrder()` that checks stock, saves the order, and captures exactly what got saved.
realWorldExample.input: Two rows from `@CsvSource`: stock of 5 (should succeed) and stock of 0 (should fail), each run as its own test.
realWorldExample.execution: `OrderService` asks the mocked `InventoryClient` for stock, decides whether to place the order, and — only on success — calls `orderRepository.save()`.
realWorldExample.output: For stock 5, `placeOrder` returns `true` and the captured `Order` has SKU `sku-1`. For stock 0, it returns `false` and `save()` is never called.
realWorldExample.explanation: One parameterized test replaces two nearly-identical test methods, and `ArgumentCaptor` proves not just that `save()` ran, but exactly what was passed to it.

comparisonTables.title: Mock vs Spy
rows: Run for real unless explicitly stubbed
Delegates to the real object's actual logic
Fully isolate the class under test
Partial mocking — fake only specific methods
Higher — can trigger real side effects if unstubbed

comparisonTables.title: @Mock vs @MockBean
rows: Plain Mockito object, no Spring involved
Replaces a real bean inside the Spring `ApplicationContext`
A loaded Spring test context, e.g. `@SpringBootTest` or `@WebMvcTest`
Fast — no context loading at all
Integration tests that need one collaborator faked

bestPractices: Keep one unit test focused on one class — mock every collaborator it depends on.
Prefer constructor injection so `@InjectMocks` can wire mocks in without reflection tricks.
Use `@ParameterizedTest` instead of copy-pasting near-identical test methods for different inputs.
commonMistakes: Mocking a class you don't own the boundary of, e.g. mocking a simple value object instead of just constructing it.
Forgetting `doReturn().when()` syntax on a `@Spy` and getting the real method's side effects by accident.
Verifying too many trivial interactions, turning the test into a rigid mirror of the implementation instead of its behavior.
interviewTips: If asked to distinguish Mock and Spy, lead with: a mock fakes everything, a spy fakes only what you stub — the rest runs for real.
Be ready to explain why `ArgumentCaptor` is needed even after `verify()` already confirms a method was called.

faq.q: Why use Mockito instead of writing fake classes by hand?
faq.a: Mockito generates the fake at runtime with one annotation, so there's no extra class to maintain, and stubbing/verification stay expressive and consistent across the codebase.

faq.q: Can a unit test use a real database?
faq.a: It shouldn't. The moment a real database, network call, or file system is involved, the test is no longer isolated — that's the job of an integration test, not a unit test.
It creates an instance of the class under test and injects the `@Mock`-annotated fields into it, preferring a constructor if one is available.

summary: Unit testing isolates one class or method using JUnit 5 as the framework and Mockito to fake its dependencies.
JUnit 5 provides annotations, assertions, and parameterized tests; Mockito provides mocks, stubs, verification, and argument capture.
A good unit test is fast, deterministic, and fails only when the class under test itself has a bug.
quickRevision: JUnit 5 = test framework; Mockito = fake dependencies. Used together for isolated unit tests.
`@Test`, `@BeforeEach`, `@BeforeAll` control lifecycle; `@DisplayName` and `@Disabled` control readability/skipping.
`@ParameterizedTest` + `@CsvSource`/`@ValueSource`/`@MethodSource` run one test body against many inputs.
`@Nested` groups related tests hierarchically while still sharing outer setup.
`when(...).thenReturn(...)` stubs a mock; `thenThrow()` and `thenAnswer()` cover exceptions and dynamic responses.
`verify(mock, times(n))` / `never()` confirm how many times an interaction happened.
`ArgumentCaptor` captures the actual argument passed to a mock to assert on its contents.
`@Spy` runs real methods unless stubbed with `doReturn().when()` — the opposite default of a mock.

beginner: What is the difference between JUnit and Mockito?
What does `@Mock` do, and what value does a fresh mock return by default?
What is the AAA pattern in a unit test?
intermediate: How would you test the same method with five different input combinations without writing five methods?
What's the difference between `verify()` and `assertEquals()` in a Mockito-based test?
When would you reach for `ArgumentCaptor` instead of just `verify()`?
advanced: Why does stubbing a `@Spy` require `doReturn().when()` instead of the usual `when().thenReturn()`?
How does `@TestInstance(Lifecycle.PER_CLASS)` change how shared state behaves between test methods?
What are the risks of over-mocking a class's collaborators in a unit test?

title: Integration Testing
overview: Integration testing checks that multiple real pieces of the system work together correctly.
Instead of faking the database or message broker, it uses a real (or near-real) one.
**Spring Boot Test** wires up real Spring context slices; **Testcontainers** supplies real infrastructure via Docker.
why: Unit tests prove each class works in isolation, but they can't prove the pieces fit together.
A repository can pass every mocked unit test and still fail against the actual SQL dialect of a real database.
Integration tests catch wiring problems, query bugs, and serialization issues that mocks are blind to.
problem: Before **Testcontainers**, integration tests either used a shared test database (flaky, stateful, hard to reset) or an in-memory substitute like H2 that doesn't behave exactly like production Postgres.
Before **Spring Boot's test slices**, every integration test loaded the *entire* application context, even to test just one controller — slow and wasteful.
Both problems needed a fix: load only what's needed, and test against infrastructure that matches production.

intro: Spring Boot Test controls how much of the app loads; Testcontainers controls what real infrastructure backs it.

topics.heading: Spring Boot Test
points: **`@SpringBootTest`** loads the full application context. `webEnvironment = RANDOM_PORT` starts a real embedded server for the test.
`@AutoConfigureMockMvc` adds `MockMvc` on top of `@SpringBootTest`, letting you send fake HTTP requests without starting an actual server.
**`@WebMvcTest`** loads only the web layer — controllers, filters, `@ControllerAdvice` — and nothing else.
The service layer is faked with `@MockBean` in a `@WebMvcTest`, keeping the test fast and focused on HTTP behavior.
**`@DataJpaTest`** configures an in-memory database and scans only `@Entity` and `@Repository` classes.
Each `@DataJpaTest` auto-rolls back its transaction after the test. `@AutoConfigureTestDatabase(replace = NONE)` swaps in a real database instead of the in-memory one.
**`@MockBean`** replaces a bean inside the `ApplicationContext` with a Mockito mock; `@SpyBean` wraps the existing real bean instead.
Both are scoped to the test class and reset between tests.
**Test slices** beyond the common ones — `@JsonTest` (Jackson serialization), `@RestClientTest` (REST clients), `@JdbcTest` (raw JDBC) — each load a minimal context for speed.

topics.heading: Testcontainers
points: **Purpose** — spin up real Docker containers for tests: PostgreSQL, MySQL, Kafka, Redis, Elasticsearch. Disposable and consistent every run.
**PostgreSQL example** — `@Container static PostgreSQLContainer<?> pg = new PostgreSQLContainer<>("postgres:16")` gets a dynamic port and an auto-created database.
**`@ServiceConnection`** (Spring Boot 3.1+) auto-configures the datasource/connection properties straight from the running container.
It replaces the older, more manual `@DynamicPropertySource` wiring.
**Reusable containers** — `.withReuse(true)` keeps a container alive between test runs instead of tearing it down each time.
Requires `testcontainers.reuse.enable=true` in `~/.testcontainers.properties` — makes local and CI runs noticeably faster.
**Kafka Testcontainer** — `KafkaContainer` (Confluent image) lets producers, consumers, and stream processing be tested against a real broker.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Test class starts"] --> B["Testcontainers launches PostgreSQL in Docker"]
    B --> C["ServiceConnection injects datasource properties"]
    C --> D["Spring context loads (full or a slice)"]
    D --> E["Test runs real queries against the container"]
    E --> F["Container disposed after test class"]
architectureFlow.caption: Testcontainers starts real infrastructure before the Spring context loads, and tears it down when the test class finishes.

simpleExample.scenario: A `@DataJpaTest` verifying `OrderRepository.findByStatus()` against a real query.
simpleExample.input: Two saved orders — one `PENDING`, one `SHIPPED` — inserted into an in-memory database configured by `@DataJpaTest`.
simpleExample.execution: `findByStatus("PENDING")` runs as a real JPA query against the in-memory database, then the transaction auto-rolls back after the test.
simpleExample.output: A list containing exactly one order, `order-1`, with status `PENDING`.
simpleExample.explanation: Unlike a unit test with a mocked repository, this proves the actual query method returns the correct rows — a real database executed it.

realWorldExample.scenario: Testing `PaymentRepository` against a real PostgreSQL container instead of an in-memory substitute, using Testcontainers.
realWorldExample.input: A `Payment` with ID `txn-1` and amount 2500, saved through the repository, with a live PostgreSQL 16 container backing the datasource.
realWorldExample.execution: Testcontainers starts a real Postgres instance in Docker, `@ServiceConnection` wires the datasource to it automatically, then the save and reload run against that real database engine.
realWorldExample.output: The reloaded `Payment` is present and its amount matches exactly what was saved — validated against real Postgres SQL semantics, not an approximation.
realWorldExample.explanation: This catches issues an in-memory H2 database would miss — Postgres-specific column types, constraints, or SQL dialect differences — while still being fully disposable and CI-safe.

comparisonTables.title: @WebMvcTest vs @SpringBootTest vs @DataJpaTest
rows: Full end-to-end flow through the app

comparisonTables.title: Mocks vs Testcontainers
rows: Real Docker containers (Postgres, Kafka, Redis, etc.)
Slower — a container has to start
Docker running on the test machine or CI runner

bestPractices: Pick the narrowest test slice that answers the question — `@WebMvcTest` for controllers, `@DataJpaTest` for repositories, `@SpringBootTest` only when you truly need the whole context.
Prefer `@ServiceConnection` over manually wiring `@DynamicPropertySource` for container-backed datasources.
Enable reusable containers locally to avoid restarting Postgres/Kafka on every test run during development.
commonMistakes: Reaching for `@SpringBootTest` by default when a lighter test slice would answer the same question faster.
Testing against an in-memory database like H2 for logic that depends on Postgres-specific behavior, then being surprised in production.
Not disposing or reusing Testcontainers properly, leaving containers running and slowing down CI.
interviewTips: If asked why Testcontainers over H2, lead with: H2 approximates a database, Testcontainers runs the actual one your production system uses.
Be ready to name the specific test slice you'd use for a given class — controller, repository, or JSON serialization — and why.

faq.q: Do I need Docker installed to use Testcontainers?
faq.a: Yes — Testcontainers launches real Docker containers, so Docker (or a compatible runtime) must be available on whatever machine or CI runner executes the tests.

faq.q: Why not just use an in-memory database like H2 for everything?
faq.a: H2 doesn't perfectly replicate Postgres or MySQL's SQL dialect, constraints, or data types, so tests can pass against H2 and still fail in production against the real engine.

faq.q: What's the difference between @MockBean and using a real dependency in an integration test?
faq.a: `@MockBean` fakes one specific collaborator inside an otherwise real Spring context — useful when you want everything real except one external system you can't run in tests, like a third-party payment gateway.

summary: Integration testing verifies that real components work together, using Spring Boot's test slices to control how much context loads and Testcontainers to supply real infrastructure.
Test slices — `@WebMvcTest`, `@DataJpaTest`, `@SpringBootTest` — trade off context size against how end-to-end the test is.
Testcontainers replaces approximations like H2 with real, disposable Docker containers for databases and brokers.
quickRevision: `@SpringBootTest` loads the full context; `@WebMvcTest` and `@DataJpaTest` load narrow slices for speed.
`@AutoConfigureMockMvc` adds MockMvc on top of `@SpringBootTest` without starting a real server.
`@DataJpaTest` auto-rolls back each test's transaction; use `replace = NONE` to point it at a real database.
`@MockBean` replaces a bean with a mock; `@SpyBean` wraps the real bean — both scoped to the test class.
Testcontainers spins up real Docker containers — Postgres, MySQL, Kafka, Redis — for integration tests.
`@ServiceConnection` (Spring Boot 3.1+) auto-wires datasource properties from a container, replacing manual `@DynamicPropertySource`.
`.withReuse(true)` plus `~/.testcontainers.properties` keeps containers alive between runs for speed.
Pick the narrowest test slice or container setup that actually answers the question being tested.

beginner: What is the difference between `@SpringBootTest` and `@WebMvcTest`?
What happens to the transaction after each `@DataJpaTest` method?
intermediate: When would you choose `@DataJpaTest` over `@SpringBootTest` for testing a repository?
How does `@ServiceConnection` simplify wiring a Testcontainers-backed datasource?
What's the tradeoff between using H2 and using a real database via Testcontainers?
advanced: How would you speed up a CI pipeline that's slow because every test spins up a fresh Testcontainers instance?
What's the difference between `@MockBean` and `@SpyBean`, and when would you pick one over the other in an integration test?
How would you structure test slices across a large codebase to keep the overall test suite fast?

title: Best Practices
overview: Writing tests that run isn't the same as writing tests that are worth keeping.
Good test design, a healthy test pyramid, and real coverage measurement all determine whether a test suite helps or slows a team down.
This section covers the habits and tools that keep a suite fast, trustworthy, and maintainable.
why: A test suite is code too, and bad test code compounds — one flaky or unclear test can waste hours of debugging time.
Teams need a shared standard for what a *good* test looks like, not just what makes the build pass.
Coverage tools and the test pyramid exist to make test quality measurable, not just a matter of opinion.
problem: Without agreed-on test design, tests end up unreadable, order-dependent, or duplicating the same assertion five different ways.
Without a test pyramid, teams either write too few tests (bugs slip through) or too many slow end-to-end tests (the ice-cream-cone anti-pattern — slow, brittle, and expensive to maintain).
Without coverage measurement, there's no objective signal for which code paths are actually exercised by any test at all.

intro: Two areas matter most: how individual tests are written, and how the whole suite is shaped and measured.

topics.heading: Test Design
points: **Naming convention** — `shouldReturnUser_whenIdExists()` or `givenValidId_whenFindById_thenReturnUser()`. The name alone should describe the scenario and expected outcome.
**AAA pattern** — Arrange (set up data and mocks), Act (call the method under test), Assert (verify results). The separation keeps tests scannable.
**One concept per test** — each test verifies one behavior. Multiple assertions are fine as long as they all check that same concept.
This avoids one failing test hiding three unrelated bugs behind a single red X.
**Test independence** — tests must not depend on execution order or shared mutable state; each test builds its own data, typically via `@BeforeEach`.
**Avoid test smells** — no branching logic (`if`/loops) inside a test, no testing private methods directly, no interdependent tests, and no tolerating flaky tests — fix them or quarantine them.

topics.heading: Test Pyramid & Coverage
points: **Test pyramid** — many fast, isolated unit tests at the base; fewer, slower integration tests in the middle; a minimal number of full end-to-end tests at the top.
Inverting this shape — few unit tests, many slow E2E tests — is the ice-cream-cone anti-pattern: slow feedback and expensive maintenance.
**JaCoCo** — a Java code coverage tool, typically run as a Maven/Gradle plugin. Reports line, branch, and instruction coverage, with configurable minimum thresholds.
**Mutation testing** — PIT (PITest) deliberately mutates the source code and checks whether the existing tests catch the change.
It measures test *quality* beyond simple line coverage — a test can execute a line and still not actually assert anything meaningful about it.
**Test data builders** — a builder pattern for constructing test objects, e.g. `TestOrderBuilder.anOrder().withStatus("PENDING").build()`. Cuts boilerplate and keeps test setup readable.
**CI integration** — run tests on every PR, fail the build on test failure or a coverage drop, and publish reports as build artifacts.
Splitting fast (unit) and slow (integration) tests into separate pipeline stages keeps feedback quick without skipping thorough checks.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Many Unit Tests - fast, isolated"] --> B["Fewer Integration Tests - slower, real dependencies"]
    B --> C["Few End-to-End Tests - slowest, full system"]
    C --> D["Confidence to ship"]
    E["Ice-cream-cone anti-pattern: shape inverted"] -.->|avoid| D
architectureFlow.caption: The test pyramid: most confidence should come from cheap, fast unit tests, with progressively fewer and slower tests above.

simpleExample.scenario: Rewriting an unclear test for `CustomerService.isEligibleForDiscount()` using the AAA pattern and a clear name.
simpleExample.input: A `Customer` with status `LOYALTY`, passed directly into `isEligibleForDiscount()` — no mocks needed since the method is pure logic.
simpleExample.execution: The three sections — arrange, act, assert — are visibly separated, and the test name states the exact scenario and expected result before you even read the body.
simpleExample.output: `isEligibleForDiscount()` returns `true`, matching what the test name promised.
simpleExample.explanation: Anyone reading just the test name and the assert line understands the requirement being verified, with no need to trace through unrelated setup.

realWorldExample.scenario: Using a test data builder plus JaCoCo/PIT in a `PaymentService` suite to keep tests readable and catch a weak assertion.
realWorldExample.input: A `Payment` built via `TestPaymentBuilder` with a suspiciously large amount, everything else defaulted to sensible values.
realWorldExample.execution: The builder hides irrelevant fields, so the test only calls out `withAmount(999_999)` — the one thing that actually matters for this scenario.
realWorldExample.output: `fraudService.isSuspicious(payment)` returns `true`. Running PIT against this suite would separately confirm that mutating the limit threshold in `FraudService` actually breaks this test — proving the assertion is meaningful, not just line coverage.
realWorldExample.explanation: The builder keeps the arrange step short and intention-revealing; PIT then verifies this test would actually catch a real regression, which JaCoCo's line coverage alone can't guarantee.

comparisonTables.title: Unit vs Integration vs E2E Testing
rows: The entire system, as a user would use it
All real, often through the actual UI/API
Only tells you something, somewhere, broke

comparisonTables.title: Line Coverage vs Mutation Testing
rows: Whether a line of code executed during tests
Whether tests actually catch a change to that code
A line can run with zero meaningful assertions on it
N/A — a surviving mutant reveals exactly that gap
Much slower — recompiles and re-runs for each mutation
A baseline gate in CI (e.g. 80% minimum)
Periodic deep quality check, not every single PR

bestPractices: Write the test name first — if you can't name the scenario clearly, the test is probably testing too much at once.
Keep the test pyramid shape: most tests at the unit level, progressively fewer as you move toward full E2E.
Treat a flaky test as a bug to fix immediately, not something to re-run until it passes.
commonMistakes: Chasing a high line-coverage percentage while leaving weak or missing assertions that mutation testing would expose.
Letting tests share mutable state or run in a required order, so the suite silently breaks when tests are reordered or run in parallel.
Writing mostly slow E2E tests because they feel more 'real', inverting the pyramid into the ice-cream-cone anti-pattern.
interviewTips: If asked about the test pyramid, be ready to explain the ice-cream-cone anti-pattern as the concrete failure mode of getting it backwards.
If asked how to judge test quality beyond coverage percentage, mention mutation testing (PIT) by name.

faq.q: Is 100% code coverage a good goal?
faq.a: Not by itself — a line can execute during a test with no meaningful assertion checking its result. High coverage with weak assertions gives false confidence; mutation testing is a better quality signal.

faq.q: Why is the ice-cream-cone anti-pattern bad if E2E tests test the whole system?
faq.a: E2E tests are slow, brittle, and hard to debug — a failure could be caused by dozens of components, so pinpointing the actual bug takes far longer than a failing unit test would.

faq.q: What's the practical benefit of a test data builder over just calling the constructor directly?
faq.a: It lets each test set only the fields relevant to its scenario, while everything else defaults sensibly — keeping the arrange step short and making the actual point of the test obvious.

summary: Good test design means clear names, the AAA pattern, one concept per test, and independence from execution order.
The test pyramid keeps the suite fast and maintainable: many unit tests, fewer integration tests, minimal E2E tests.
JaCoCo measures how much code ran under test; PIT (mutation testing) measures whether the tests would actually catch a real bug.
quickRevision: Name tests by scenario and expected outcome, e.g. `shouldReturnUser_whenIdExists()`.
AAA pattern: Arrange, Act, Assert — keeps every test readable in the same shape.
One test verifies one concept; independent tests never rely on execution order or shared state.
Avoid test smells: no logic in tests, no testing private methods, no flaky tests left unfixed.
Test pyramid: many unit, fewer integration, minimal E2E. Inverted shape = ice-cream-cone anti-pattern.
JaCoCo reports line/branch/instruction coverage; set minimum thresholds in the build.
PIT (mutation testing) mutates code to check whether tests actually catch the change — reveals weak assertions.
Test data builders reduce setup boilerplate and keep the arrange step focused on what matters.
CI should fail on test failure or coverage drop, with fast (unit) and slow (integration) pipelines separated.

beginner: What does the AAA pattern stand for, and why does it help?
What is the test pyramid, and why does it have that shape?
intermediate: Why can 100% line coverage still leave real bugs uncaught?
What's the ice-cream-cone anti-pattern, and what problems does it cause?
How does a test data builder improve test readability compared to calling constructors directly?
advanced: How would you convince a team to fix a flaky test instead of just re-running the CI job?
What's the tradeoff of running mutation testing (PIT) on every PR versus periodically?
How would you structure CI pipelines to keep fast feedback while still running slower integration and E2E suites?
